Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic this semester. I tried to set aside specific blocks for studying, but with 13 online courses going on simultaneously, I found myself constantly jumping between subjects. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time creating mind maps and diagrams, which took longer than just reading. I aimed for about 25 hours a week, but those hours were scattered across late nights and random free periods between other commitments.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due. I'd have these intense bursts of activity right before deadlines, then quieter periods where I'd barely touch certain courses. My extracurriculars also threw off any consistent schedule I tried to maintain. Some weeks I was organized, other weeks I was just surviving and putting out fires wherever they popped up.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline notification or realizing I'd fallen behind that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd get an email about new content being posted and that would prompt me. Honestly, anxiety was a big driver too—I'd log in just to check if I'd missed something important, even if I didn't end up doing much while I was there.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging in became automatic, usually when I was on top of things early in the semester. I'd check in during my morning coffee before other activities. But that routine fell apart once the workload from all my courses piled up. The CBT sessions I attended actually helped me try to rebuild some structure, but it was a constant struggle to maintain consistency.
Q5: The video lectures were probably the most useful for me because I learn best visually. I watched almost two hours worth and would pause to sketch out concepts. The discussion boards were helpful when peers explained things differently than the instructor—sometimes that alternative perspective clicked better for me. Having everything downloadable was also crucial since I often studied offline.
Q6: I'll admit I ignored a lot of the reading materials and some of the supplementary resources. When you're juggling 13 courses, you start triaging what's absolutely necessary versus what's nice-to-have. Some discussion threads felt like busywork, so I'd skim them rather than engage deeply. I also avoided anything that seemed redundant to what I'd already learned from videos.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to assignments or exams. If I could see the practical payoff, I was in. Visual resources like diagrams, videos, or interactive elements grabbed my attention more than walls of text. I also valued anything that helped reduce my anxiety about upcoming assessments—practice problems were gold for that reason.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford most of the time. Required ones got prioritized out of necessity, even when I was exhausted. With optional stuff, I'd think 'I'll get to it later' but later never came. There was also less guilt attached to skipping optional activities, which made them easier to deprioritize when stress was high.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and videos, then work through them on my tablet without internet. I used physical notebooks constantly for drawing diagrams and connecting concepts visually. I also relied on YouTube videos and external websites when course explanations weren't clicking for me.
Q10: Most of my deep learning happened away from the LMS. I'd download materials in one quick session, then spend hours working through them at a café or in the library with everything offline. My mind maps and study notes were extensive, but none of that showed up anywhere. Group study sessions with classmates also contributed a lot to my understanding.
Q11: All the time, actually. I'd log in, download everything available for a module, and then not touch the LMS again for days while I worked through it all. The videos especially—I'd watch them once online, then have them saved locally to rewatch specific parts. My LMS activity probably looked minimal during some of my most productive study periods.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when everything seemed due at once. My anxiety spiked and I found myself paralyzed, unable to start anything because everything felt overwhelming. The stress made it hard to focus even when I did sit down to study. I also got sick for a week which threw off my already fragile routine completely.
Q13: Massively. When I was fatigued or stressed, I'd log into the LMS and just stare at the screen without absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities from extracurriculars and other courses meant the LMS often got whatever energy I had left, which wasn't much. Confusion about course expectations also made me hesitant to engage—I didn't want to waste time on the wrong things.
Q14: Yeah, this happened more than I'd like to admit. I'd have a video playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about other deadlines or just mentally checked out from exhaustion. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just to feel like I was doing something. It was engagement in appearance only.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even important ones. The anxiety made starting feel impossible—I'd build things up in my head until they seemed insurmountable. I knew procrastination made everything worse, but in the moment, avoidance felt like relief. The CBT sessions helped me recognize this pattern, but breaking it was another matter entirely.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire LMS usage. I'd have low activity for days, then a massive spike right before something was due. It wasn't healthy, but it's how I functioned under the pressure of so many courses. Deadlines created urgency that cut through my anxiety paralysis and forced action, even if it was last-minute.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Positive feedback motivated me to engage more with that particular course. But critical feedback sometimes increased my anxiety and made me avoid that course for a while. I tried to use feedback constructively, but when stress was high, it was hard not to take it personally and spiral a bit.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and overwhelming. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me something to work toward, which I needed. But with 13 courses, even well-structured content became too much to track. I appreciated when instructors front-loaded materials so I could work ahead during calmer periods.
Q19: The visual resources and downloadable content made engagement easier for someone like me. What made it harder was when important information was buried in long text documents or when navigation was confusing. Having too many small tasks also overwhelmed me—I preferred fewer, more substantial assignments that I could focus on deeply.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually completing work. Or maybe they're trying to understand material but struggling, so they keep returning without making progress. It could also be someone who's engaged mentally but hasn't translated that into submitted assignments yet—the work is happening, just not visibly.
Q21: That actually describes me pretty well. My attendance and LMS activity were low, but I still passed because I was learning effectively offline. I'd download materials, study intensively on my own, and show up for exams prepared. The LMS numbers don't capture the hours I spent with downloaded content or my own notes and diagrams.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you log in and what you click on, which might indicate deadline-driven behavior or consistent engagement. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of attention during online sessions, and emotional state. They can't tell if someone watched a video while fully focused or while mentally elsewhere.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, making connections between concepts. For me, it involves creating visual representations that help things stick. True engagement also means wrestling with difficult content rather than just skimming past it.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my anxiety affected my engagement or how hard I worked to push through it. It wouldn't capture the extensive offline studying I did or the peer discussions outside the platform. It also wouldn't show that despite my low visible activity, I was genuinely trying to learn and grow—I just did it in ways that don't leave digital footprints.
